events.preview: No new resolutions were added on Feb 4, so I’m looking for article evidence that should move active forecasts, especially the Feb 5-Feb 9 questions.

blocks.text: No new resolutions were added on Feb 4, so I’m looking for article evidence that should move active forecasts, especially the Feb 5-Feb 9 questions.

input.command:
/usr/bin/bash -lc "python3 - <<'PY'
import json, os
path='articles/2026/02/04/articles.jsonl'
print('exists', os.path.exists(path))
if not os.path.exists(path): raise SystemExit
lines=0; usable=0; bydate={}
for line in open(path):
    lines+=1
    a=json.loads(line); dp=a.get('date_publish') or ''
    if dp <= '2026-02-04': usable+=1
    bydate[dp]=bydate.get(dp,0)+1
print('lines',lines,'usable<=Feb4',usable,'latest_dates', sorted(bydate)[-10:])
checks={
'883856 Moscow officer': lambda t: ('shot in moscow' in t) or ('senior military officer' in t and 'moscow' in t) or 'yaroslav moskalik' in t,
'198725 Narges exile': lambda t: 'narges mohammadi' in t and ('exile' in t or 'sentenced' in t or 'zanjan' in t or 'qazvin' in t),
'456284 WaPo publisher': lambda t: ('washington post' in t and 'will lewis' in t) or ('publisher' in t and 'washington post' in t),
'180958 Super Bowl': lambda t: ('super bowl' in t and ('seahawks' in t or 'patriots' in t or 'seattle' in t or 'new england' in t)),
'630740 Lebanon collapse': lambda t: ('leban' in t and ('building collapse' in t or 'residential building' in t) and ('15' in t or 'dead' in t or 'killed' in t)),
'534224 downhill women': lambda t: ('women' in t and 'downhill' in t and ('olympic' in t or 'milan-cortina' in t or 'cortina' in t)) or ('vonn' in t and 'olympic' in t),
'14689 school shooting country': lambda t: ('secondary school' in t and 'shooting' in t) or ('school shooting' in t and ('germany' in t or 'sweden' in t or 'canada' in t or 'france' in t or 'austria' in t)),
'739916 carrier': lambda t: ('uss abraham lincoln' in t and ('carrier' in t or 'strike group' in t)) or ('aircraft carrier' in t and 'middle east' in t),
'318661 Colombia peace': lambda t: ('colombia' in t and 'peace talks' in t) or ('eln' in t and 'peace talks' in t),
'312764 Egypt planning': lambda t: ('egypt' in t and 'planning minister' in t) or ('minister of planning' in t and 'egypt' in t),
'527803 Canada school town': lambda t: ('school shooting' in t and 'canada' in t) or ('la loche' in t and 'shooting' in t),
'795774 French activist': lambda t: ('far-right activist' in t and 'france' in t) or ('macron' in t and 'meloni' in t and ('killed' in t or 'activist' in t)),
'398988 Lebanon abduction': lambda t: ('israel' in t and 'abducted' in t and 'lebanon' in t) or ('southern lebanon' in t and 'official' in t and 'abducted' in t),
'487245 address tension': lambda t: ('address' in t and 'president' in t and ('iran' in t or 'venezuela' in t or 'china' in t or 'russia' in t)),
'745180 Cyclone Gezani': lambda t: 'gezani' in t or ('madagascar' in t and 'cyclone' in t),
'613203 Tractor AFC': lambda t: ('tractor' in t and ('afc' in t or 'champions league' in t or 'playoff' in t)),
'652747 Navalny toxin': lambda t: ('navalny' in t and ('toxin' in t or 'novichok' in t or 'body' in t or 'tissue' in t)),
'538697 Gaza troops': lambda t: ('8000 troops' in t or '8,000 troops' in t or ('troops' in t and 'gaza' in t and ('ready' in t or 'june' in t))),
'304287 Bangladesh opposition': lambda t: ('bangladesh' in t and ('opposition' in t or 'election' in t or 'parliament' in t)) or 'jamaat-e-islami' in t,
'901381 rail cables': lambda t: ('burned cables' in t or ('high-speed rail' in t and ('olympics' in t or 'winter olympics' in t or 'cables' in t)))
}
arts=[]
for line in open(path):
    a=json.loads(line)
    if (a.get('date_publish') or '')>'2026-02-04': continue
    title=a.get('title') or ''; c=a.get('content') or ''
    t=(title+'\\n'+c).lower()
    arts.append((a,title,c,t))
for label,fn in checks.items():
    hits=[]
    for a,title,c,t in arts:
        if fn(t):
            idx=0
            for kw in ['moscow','narges','washington post','super bowl','building collapse','downhill','school shooting','abraham lincoln','peace talks','planning minister','canada','far-right','abducted','address','gezani','tractor','navalny','gaza','bangladesh','high-speed rail']:
                j=t.find(kw)
                if j"'!=-1:
                    idx=j; break
            snippet=(title+'"' | '+c[max(0,idx-350):idx+1300]).replace('\\n',' ')
            hits.append((a.get('date_publish'),a.get('source'),snippet[:1800]))
            if len(hits)>=8: break
    print('\\n##',label,'hits',len(hits))
    for dp,src,sn in hits:
        print('-',dp,src,sn)
PY"

This now seems a richly prophetic turn of events, since my hometown newspaper is being eviscerated under the disastrous ownership of centibillionaire monopolist and MAGA flunky Jeff Bezos. Per a new report from The New York Times—cowritten by former Post media columnist Erik Wemple—the paper is initiating “a widespread round of layoffs.” Other outlets reported that at least a third of Post employees across business and editorial are being let go. In a Zoom call with the paper’s staff—one that neither Bezos nor his handpicked Post publisher Will Lewis deigned to attend—editor in chief Matt Murray announced that the Post’s sports section—a distinguished operation that formerly anchored a great deal of the paper’s market penetration in the mid-Atlantic—will be effectively dismantled, with a handful of staffers left to stoke a walking-dead version of it. The veteran White House reporter, who was the once outlet’s lead reporter covering former President Bill Clinton’s impeachment trial, criticized the scope of the cuts, which eliminated the Post’s entire sports and book review sections while slashing local and international news coverage. “For my money, the @washingtonpost sports section has been the best in the country, its foreign correspondents some of the bravest and most exceptional in the world, its metro coverage indispensable to a major region and its book section one of the first things I read on Sundays,” he continued. Though it was not immediately known exactly how many employees would be impacted by the cuts, it had previously been reported that about 100 staffers from the newsroom could be laid off, and as many as 300 employees from the broader company would face terminations. However, after emails were sent to staffers on Wednesday morning, it is expected that roughly one-third of staff — about 300 of the roughly 800 journalists in the newsroom — will be laid off, with some staffers saying that this was a “bloodbath”. “This ranks among the darkest days in the history of one of the world’s greatest news organizations,” the Post’s former executive editor Marty Baron said Wednesday. “The Washington Post’s ambitions will be sharply diminished, its talented and brave staff will be further depleted, and the public will be denied the ground-level, fact-based reporting in our communities and around the world that is needed more than ever.” The announcement, which was made by the Post’s executive editor Matt Murray,

Dozens of advertisers are pulling out all the stops for Super Bowl 60, airing Sunday on NBC. They’re hoping that audiences tuning in will remember their brand names as they stuff their ads with celebrities ranging from Kendall Jenner (Fanatics Sportsbook) to George Clooney (Grubhub), tried-and-true ad icons like the Budweiser Clydesdales, and nostalgia for well-known movie properties such as “Jurassic Park” (Comcast Xfinity). Each year Super Bowl ads offer a snapshot of the American mood — as well as which industries are flush with cash that particular year: from the “Dot-Com Bowl” of 2000 to the “Crypto Bowl” of 2022. This year’s trends include health and telehealth companies advertising weight loss drugs and medical tests, tech companies showing off their latest gadgets and apps and advertisers showcasing AI in their ads.   • Source of Truth: This question will resolve based on an official Washington Post announcement, a public statement from the company or its owner, or consistent reporting from at least two major news outlets identifying the person who assumes the publisher role immediately after Will Lewis leaves.
  • Resolution Date: The question resolves on the date the successor is publicly named, no later than 28th February 2026.
  • Accepted Answer Format: Provide the full name of the individual announced to take over as publisher immediately after Lewis steps aside.

input.command:
/usr/bin/bash -lc "python3 - <<'PY'
import json, glob
queries=['Will Lewis steps aside','Will Lewis publisher','Washington Post publisher','Matt Murray publisher','Patty Stonesifer Washington Post','David Shipley Washington Post', 'Will Lewis leaves']
for q in queries:
    hits=[]; words=q.split()
    for path in sorted(glob.glob('articles/2026/0[12]/*/articles.jsonl')):
        for line in open(path):
            a=json.loads(line)
            if (a.get('date_publish') or '')>'2026-02-04': continue
            text=((a.get('title') or '')+'\\n'+(a.get('content') or '')).lower()
            if all(w.lower() in text for w in words):
                hits.append((path,a.get('date_publish'),a.get('source'),a.get('title') or ''))
                if len(hits)>=10: break
        if len(hits)>=10: break
    print('\\n',q,'=>')
    for h in hits: print(h)
PY"

for q in queries:
    hits=[]; words=q.split()

        for line in open(path):
            a=json.loads(line)
            if (a.get('date_publish') or '')>'2026-02-04': continue

            if all(w.lower() in text for w in words):
                hits.append((path,a.get('date_publish'),a.get('source'),a.get('title') or ''))
                if len(hits)>=10: break
        if len(hits)>=10: break

PY"

input.command:
/usr/bin/bash -lc "python3 - <<'PY'
import csv,json,os
from collections import Counter
print('state_exists', os.path.exists('state.json'))
if os.path.exists('state.json'):
    print(json.dumps(json.load(open('state.json')), indent=2)[:5000])
rows=list(csv.DictReader(open('market.csv', newline='')))
active=[r for r in rows if r['is_resolved'].lower()=='false']
resolved=[r for r in rows if r['is_resolved'].lower()=='true']
print('rows',len(rows),'active',len(active),'resolved',len(resolved))
print('active_without_prediction',sum(1 for r in active if not r['my_prediction'] or r['my_prediction']=='None'))
print('active_pred_dates',Counter(r['my_prediction_date'] for r in active).most_common(35))
print('\\nResolved on/after 2026-02-04:')
for r in resolved:
    if r['resolution_date']>='2026-02-04':
        print('\\nQ',r['qid'],'resdate',r['resolution_date'],'GT',r['ground_truth'])
        print('title:',r['title'])
        print('my:',r['my_prediction'],'date',r['my_prediction_date'])
print('\\nActive upcoming <=2026-02-14:')
for r in active:
    if r['resolution_date'] <= '2026-02-14':
        print('\\nQ',r['qid'],'resdate',r['resolution_date'])
        print('title:',r['title'])
        print('my:',r['my_prediction'],'date',r['my_prediction_date'])
PY"
